design_philosophy.core_loop: Explore open-world city, defeat gang members in combat, collect loot, purchase stat upgrades and techniques at shops, face gang leaders as bosses, progress toward River City High School
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource management (money/food for stat growth) vs. combat difficulty; exploration freedom vs. directed goal to rescue Cyndi
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: High school students fighting their way through an urban environment as underdogs, becoming stronger through training and equipment rather than narrative progression alone
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Revolutionary non-linear open-world design for beat-em-ups; RPG stat/upgrade system embedded into beat-em-up combat; persistent character progression via password system; sandbox exploration where player chooses which gangs to fight and in what order

difficulty.lives_system: No traditional lives system; player is defeated when health depletes, but can continue from shops via password system
difficulty.continues_system: Password system allows saving character stats, skills, money, items, and defeated bosses for reload; no hard continue limit
difficulty.checkpointing: Shops act as safe zones where players restore health via food and spa treatments; password system enables soft checkpointing of progress
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency: player can choose route through gangs, grind stats/money before facing difficult enemies, purchase specific techniques to counter enemy types; difficulty reduced from 3 settings to 2 (removed easiest mode in localization)

progression.upgrade_system: Purchase books for fighting techniques (Grand Slam, Stone Hands, Dragon Feet); buy food items and spa treatments to increase permanent stats (Punch, Kick, Toughness); collect dropped money from defeated enemies
progression.unlock_mechanism: Gangs must be defeated to progress; each gang has unique visual/behavioral characteristics; defeating gang leaders opens new areas; nine gangs total to encounter
replayability_hooks: Password system enables character persistence across playthroughs with different stat builds
Open-world structure allows different routes through gang encounters
Multiple difficulty settings change enemy behavior
2-player cooperative mode (removed PvP-damage mode in localization)
Stat investment creates incentive for 'optimized' playthroughs

world_building_techniques: Gang visual identity through t-shirt colors and attack patterns
Environmental storytelling via gang territories and shop placement
Character dialogue with humor and personality (localized/Americanized)
Rescue mission framing (Cyndi kidnapped by Slick) provides narrative thrust
Original Japanese version featured school uniforms and inter-school gang dynamics; localization reframed as street gangs in American context

boss_design.philosophy: Gang leaders serve as boss encounters with escalating challenge; bosses emphasize pattern recognition and stat advantage gained through prior grinding
notable_bosses: Slick/Yamada (final boss, intended to have telekinesis ability to throw all weapons—unused in NES, realized in GBA remake)

faction_indicators: Gang membership signaled by t-shirt color
Gang leader hierarchy and territory control
School-based social structure (original Famicom context: inter-school rivalries; localized version: urban street gangs)
Player character choice (Alex/Kunio vs. Ryan/Riki) affects story outcomes
danger_signals: Encountering gang members indicates territory control
Gang leader confrontation marks escalation of threat
Increasing enemy density and stat requirements signal progression toward final confrontation
environmental_storytelling: Shop placement and NPC positioning indicate safe zones vs. hostile territories
Gang member uniform colors distinguish rival factions in shared spaces
River City geography (bridge, mall, school) organizes narrative progression
Unused graphics of Roxy (Hasebe) showing emotion and movement hints at romantic subplots and character depth beyond final implementation
